title: Flow and emissions in a dual-stage counter-rotating nozzle: Effects of
text:
Research Paper
Flow and emissions in a dual-stage counter-rotating nozzle: Effects of 
swirler structure and air distribution
Ruolin Zhao a, Wenjun Kong a,b,c,*
a School of Astronautics, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, China
b National Key Laboratory of Aerospace Liquid Propulsion, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, China
c Beijing Key Laboratory of System Design for Reusable Launch Vehicle, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, China
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Dual-stage counter-rotating nozzle
Swirler structure
Air distribution
Central toroidal recirculation zone
NOx emissions
A B S T R A C T
To reduce CO and NOx emissions in gas turbines, this study investigated a novel dual-stage counter-rotating 
nozzle based on lean direct injection technology, focusing on the effects of swirler structure and air distribution 
through numerical simulations. The results were validated using particle image velocimetry and emission ex­
periments. A systematic analysis reveals that increasing the inner swirl blade angle reduces the axial velocity 
while enhancing the radial and tangential velocities, thereby increasing the swirl intensity, expanding the central 
toroidal recirculation zone (CTRZ), and shortening the kerosene penetration depth. CO and NOx emissions 
initially decrease and then increase, with optimal performance observed at an angle of 39◦. Conversely, 
increasing the outer swirl blade angle weakens swirl intensity, reduces the volume of CTRZ, increases NOx 
emissions, and decreases CO emissions, with optimal performance occurring between 40◦and 50◦. The direction 
of the mixed airflow rotation aligns with the stage that has the higher airflow ratio. When the inner-to-outer air 
flow rate ratio approaches 1:1, the CTRZ disappears. The optimal performance is achieved at a 2:1 ratio, with CO 
and NOx emissions of 4.73 ppm and 17.38 ppm, respectively. The volume of CTRZ and recirculation rate show a 
linear correlation, while heat release and combustion products reduce the volume under hot state. Combustion is 
achieved when the dimensionless CTRZ volume exceeds 0.2 under the cold state. This work can provide technical 
support and new research insights for low-emission gas turbine combustion chambers.
1. Introduction
Gas turbine engines, which efficiently convert chemical energy to 
mechanical energy, play a critical role in the aviation, marine, and 
power generation industries. However, their emissions—primarily car­
bon monoxide (CO), nitrogen oxides (NOx), and unburned hydrocar­
bons (UHC)—pose significant environmental challenges [1]. As 
technology advances and living standards improve, the emission control 
requirements for gas turbine engines, particularly with respect to NOx 
emissions, have become increasingly stringent [2]. Therefore, there is an 
urgent need to develop low-emission combustion chamber technologies 
[3].
In gas turbine engine combustion chambers, NOx is primarily 
generated through high-temperature reactions of N2, forming thermal 
NOx according to the Zeldovich mechanism [4]. Therefore, reducing 
NOx emissions depends on lowering the local high temperatures within 
the combustion chamber. Among various low-emission combustion 
chamber technologies, lean direct injection (LDI) stands out due to ad­
vantages such as a reduced risk of flashback, excellent combustion sta­
bility, and strong development potential [5–7].
A traditional LDI nozzle consists of a single-stage swirler, nozzle, and 
Venturi tube. The swirling airflow generated by the swirler interacts 
with fuel droplets through the Venturi tube, enhancing atomization, 
accelerating fuel–air mixing, and stabilizing flame. This process lowers 
local high temperatures and consequently reduces thermal NOx emis­
sions [8,9].
Extensive research has been conducted on single-stage LDI nozzles, 
including swirler structure, Venturi tube geometry, combustor di­
mensions, atomization performance, and flow-spray-combustion 
coupling [10,11]. For multi-nozzle LDI systems, research focuses on 
array configurations, such as 2 × 2 and 3 × 3 arrangements, and swirl 
direction effects on flow and emissions 12,13,14.
Regarding the flow characteristics, Fu et al. [15] conducted 3D 
Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) experiments and found that the

interaction between adjacent swirler outlet regions in a multi-point LDI 
system significantly enhanced turbulent kinetic energy, leading to the 
formation of a unique inter-stage vortex chain. Twarog et al. [16]
investigated the flow characteristics of the counter-rotating multi- 
swirler (CTS) using time-resolved particle image velocimetry (TR-PIV) 
to quantify the velocity and flow structures of the non-reacting flow 
field. This study improved the understanding of the flow behavior in a 
multi-swirler. Shen et al. [17] employed large eddy simulation (LES) to 
examine the unsteady characteristics of swirling flow in an LDI 
combustor. They observed that the swirling flow exhibited multiple jet- 
like features, which interacted with central toroidal recirculation zone 
(CTRZ) to form a “starfish” shape. Additionally, both the jet diffusion 
angle and the outer recirculation zone were significantly increased.
Significant progress has been made in optimizing fuel mixing. Heath 
et al. [8] employed computational modeling to quantify geometric 
sensitivities. Their results demonstrated that geometric modifications 
significantly alter the axial flow field, including axial velocity profiles, 
turbulent kinetic energy distribution, temperature gradients, and fuel 
dispersion. Carreres et al. [18] conducted LES on an LDI nozzle, 
revealing that fewer swirler blades or larger blade angles enhance the 
recirculation zone, thereby improving atomization and mixing 
processes.
In terms of combustion impact, Broatch et al. [19] employed LES to 
investigate the flow characteristics of liquid sprays in an LDI burner. 
Their findings highlighted the critical role of droplet distribution in the 
combustion process. The study emphasized the importance of the 
rotating vortex core and turbulence structures in the fuel–air mixing 
process and investigated the interaction between the spray and vortices. 
In a follow-up study, Broatch et al. [20] analyzed combustion noise in a 
cyclone-stabilized LDI burner, discovering that noise generation is 
strongly linked to the precessing vortex core (PVC) and vortex break­
down bubble (VBB). Patel et al. [10] applied LES to examine the in­
teractions between spray, turbulence, and flame in an LDI combustor. 
The study found that under heat release conditions, VBB was smaller and 
exhibited stronger reverse flow, while PVC significantly enhanced the 
breakup process.
El-Asrag et al. [21] used LES to investigate the radiation-spray 
coupling effects in an LDI combustor. Their study found that combus­
tion altered the shape and structure of the CTRZ, making it more 
compact along its length while increasing its lateral diameter. Bonefaˇci´c 
et al. [22] optimized fuel oil spray combustion in a 7 MW industrial 
furnace, concluding that lean air–fuel mixtures and high swirl numbers 
promote complete combustion and lower emissions, while moderate 
swirl numbers and narrow spray angles increase heating output but may 
shorten flames. Ibrahim et al. [23] studied the effect of different atom­
izing gases o

swirler height is 10 mm. The pressure-atomizing nozzle is a Danfoss 
model with a hollow-cone spray type, a spray cone angle of 60◦, and a 
flow coefficient of 0.3. The converging section of the Venturi tube has an 
angle of 30◦, and the diverging section has an angle of 45◦.
The confined space is a rectangular chamber with a 60 mm × 60 mm 
square cross-sectional area and a length of 300 mm. One end of the 
confined space is connected to the outlet of the Venturi tube, and the 
other end is connected to an exhaust hood. The fluid domain, formed by 
the nozzle and confined space, is shown in Fig. 2(b). The center of the 
outlet plane of the Venturi tube is defined as the origin, with this plane 
corresponding to the y = 0 mm plane in the figure. The positive direction 
of the y-axis is directed towards the outlet of the confined space.
This study investigates the impact of swirler blade angles and air 
distribution on the flow field and emissions in confined spaces. Specif­
ically, it examines the inner swirl blade angle (a), outer swirl blade angle 
(b), and the inner-to-outer air flow rate ratio (Ra). The experimental 
conditions are set as follows: environmental pressure of 101.325 kPa, air 
temperature of 297 K, total air flow rate of 2.93 g⋅s−1, kerosene mass 
flow rate of 0.167 g⋅s−1, and an equivalence ratio of 0.83.
The study parameters are provided in Table 1, with the baseline 
conditions (bolded in the table) corresponding to an inner swirl blade 
angle of 39◦, an outer swirl blade angle of 45◦, and an inner

solution framework. Kerosene (RP-3) is selected as the fuel, with a 
skeletal reaction mechanism comprising a 50-species, 90-step reaction 
that includes NOx formation [36]. Fuel injection is modeled using the 
Discrete Phase Model (DPM) with a 60◦atomization cone angle, hollow 
cone type, and a droplet size distribution following the R-R distribution, 
and a Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) of 35 μm. The solution is obtained 
using a COUPLED pressure–velocity coupling scheme, discretizing the 
pressure term with the PRESTO! scheme, and other terms with second- 
order accuracy.
The boundary conditions for the fluid domain in the numerical 
simulation are shown in Fig. 5. Both the inner and outer swirl air inlets 
are ring-shaped and modeled as mass flow inlets. Kerosene is injected at 
the center of the pressure atomization nozzle’s conical section, with the 
injection directed along the positive y-axis. The combustion-generated 
exhaust gases are discharged from the rear square cross-section of the 
confined space, which is set as a pressure outlet at 101.325 kPa. The 
remaining unmarked surfaces are defined as wall boundary conditions.

2.3.3. Discrete phase
This study involves the heat and mass transfer processes between the 
gas and liquid phases. Tracking individual droplets is difficult due to the 
large number of droplets. Therefore, the DPM is employed to model 
kerosene injection. The mass, force, and energy balance equations are 
solved for each particle parcel, rather than for each individual droplet.
The DPM predicts the motion trajectory of discrete phase particles in 
the continuous phase by integrating the force balance on the particles.

3.2. Comparison of experimental and simulation results
This study uses experimental data to validate the accuracy of the 
numerical simulations. Due to the limitations of the experimental setup, 
cold-state PIV and hot-state emission experiments were conducted under 
baseline conditions, providing cold-state velocity field data and hot- 
state emission data, respectively.
As shown in Fig. 8, the comparison of the radial distribution of ve­
locity-y at axial distances of 5 mm and 30 mm and the axial distribution 
of velocity-y along the y-axis in the confined space under cold condi­
tions, reveals that the numerical simulation results are in good agree­
ment with the PIV measurements. This indicates that the numerical 
simulation method used is capable of accurately predicting the velocity 
distribution.
To validate the accuracy of the emission predictions in the numerical 
simulations, a 13-hole collection rake was positioned 5 cm above the 
center of the confined space outlet to collect exhaust gases. CO, NOx

concentrations, and combustion efficiency were measured using a Testo 
340 flue gas analyzer. Sampling lasted for 1 min, and three trials were 
conducted to calculate the average and standard deviation. The results 
are presented in Table 3.
The numerical simulation results show that NOx emissions are 
slightly higher, while CO emissions are slightly lower. This discrepancy 
arises because radiation heat transfer from the confined space to the 
external mesh is not considered in the simulation. The confined space, 
constructed from quartz glass, radiates heat outward. As a result, the 
simulation underestimates heat loss through the walls, leading to a slight 
increase in temperature within the confined space. This causes a slight 
rise in NOx emissions and a reduction in CO emissions. However, the 
overall deviation is minor, and the results are considered to meet the 
accuracy requirements.
4. Results and discussions
To thoroughly analyze the impact of parameter changes on the flow 
field velocity, this study focused on extracting the distributions of radial 
velocity (Velocity-x), axial velocity (Velocity-y), and tangential velocity 
(Velocity-z) along the radial direction at an axial position of 20 mm. 
Additionally, the axial velocity (Velocity-y) distribution along the axis 
was examined. Specifically, the directions of the velocities are defined as 
follows: radial velocity is positive in the outward radial direction, axial 
velocity is positive toward the exit of the confined space along the 
central axis, and tangential velocity is positive in the direction consistent 
with the inner swirl flow. The precise locations for data extraction are 
shown in Fig. 9, designated as “Data Sampling Location 1″ and ”Data 
Sampling Location 2″.
In the LDI combustor, the CTRZ produced by the swirling flow is a 
key characteristic of the flow field. It enhances the mixing of fuel and air, 
significantly contributing to flame stability. This study adopts the defi­
nition by El-Asrag [21], where the region enclosed by the iso-surface 
with zero axial velocity along the axis is considered the CTRZ, while the 
region at the bottom edge of the confined space is defined as the CRZ. 
Consequently, this phenomenon is of considerable interest in both cold 
and hot flow field analyses. The iso-surface of zero axial velocity and 
streamline distribution are shown in Fig. 10. It is evident that the dual- 
stage counter-rotating nozzle induces swirling flow within the confined 
space, resulting in both a CTRZ and a CRZ.
To quantify the impact of parameter changes on the CTRZ, Vs is 
defined as the volume of the CTRZ from 0 to 60 mm, measured from the 
base of the confined space. By comparing it with the volume of the 
confined space Vc (60 mm × 60 mm × 60 mm in this study), a dimen­
sionless processing is applied, defining av as the dimensionless CTRZ

recirculation rate (ηq) under both cold and hot states is demonstrated in

progressively shifts forward as a increases.
The variations of CO and NOx emissions with a are presented in 
Fig. 15. The results demonstrate that as a increases, the overall swirl 
intensity of the airflow increases, leading to an initial decrease followed 
by an increase in both CO and NOx emissions. Notably, NOx emissions 
reach their minimum value of 17.38 ppm at a of 39◦, while CO emissions 
reach their lowest value of 4.52 ppm at an inner blade angle of 42◦. 
Current emission regulations impose more stringent limits on NOx. 
Therefore, within the parameters examined in this study, the optimal 
value of a is identified as 39◦. This is likely because the swirl intensity 
within this range is optimal, leading to stronger interactions between 
kerosene droplets and the airflow and a more uniform distribution of 
vaporized kerosene. Furthermore, the CTRZ contributes to stable and 
complete combustion, with a more uniform temperature distribution, 
leading to lower CO and NOx emissions.
4.2. Effect of the outer swirl blade angles
The outer swirl blade angle (b) has a significant impact on the swirl 
intensity of the outer swirl airflow. To investigate this effect, the outer 
blade angle was varied under baseline conditions for a detailed analysis.
The morphology of the CTRZ under both cold and hot states for 
different b is illustrated in Fig. 16. The results show that as b increases, 
the size of the CTRZ decreases. Under the cold state, the length of the 
CTRZ shortens and gradually adopts a spindle-like shape. Due to com­
bustion reactions, the size of the CTRZ under the hot state is smaller than

outer air flow rate ratio is 2:1, and the swirl airflow in the confined 
space is primarily controlled by the inner airflow. Since the inner and 
outer swirl flows are in opposite directions, increasing b enhances the 
outer swirl intensity, which weakens the overall swirl intensity and re­
duces the size of the CTRZ.
The radial, axial, and tangential velocity distributions along the 
radial direction at an axial position of 20 mm, as well as the axial ve­

with notable differences in the morphology of the CTRZ and the velocity 
distribution patterns.

velocity is relatively low, peaking at a non-dimensional radial distance

airflow but weakens the overall swirl intensity after mixing, thereby 
reducing the CTRZ width. Under hot state, the effect of b is mitigated by 
the heat release from combustion, which causes gas expansion in the 
confined space, elevates the axial velocity, and reduces the overall swirl 
intensity, thus shortening the width of the CTRZ.
The changes in tangential velocity along the radial direction in both 
the cold and hot states are shown in Figs. 17(e) and 17(f). As shown, 
with an increase in b, the tangential velocity decreases but remains 
positive. This indicates that while the increased outer swirl flow in­
tensity weakens the overall swirl strength after mixing, the direction of 
the airflow still matches the inner swirl flow.
The axial velocity distribution along the centerline under both the 
cold and hot states is shown in Figs. 17(g) and 17(h). The trends in axial 
velocity distribution are consistent in both the cold and hot states for all 
values of b. The axial velocity progressively increases as the value of b 
rises. Combined with Fig. 16, it can be concluded that the length of the 
CTRZ decreases with increasing b under both cold and hot states.
The effects of b on the dimensionless CTRZ volume (av) and the 
recirculation rate (ηq) are shown in Fig. 18. Under the cold state, as b 
increases, the overall swirl intensity of the airflow diminishes, leading to 
a gradual reduction in both av and ηq. However, when b exceeds 50◦, as

4.3. Effect of the inner-to-outer air flow rate ratios
In this study, the total air flow rate remains constant, and the inner- 
to-outer air flow rate ratio (Ra) influences the interactions between the 
inner swirl flow, outer swirl flow, and kerosene. This ultimately affects 
the velocity of the mixed swirling flow, the distribution of vaporized 
kerosene, the temperature profile, and the pollutant emissions at the 
outlet of the confined space. Under baseline conditions, the effects of 
varying Ra on these results were systematically investigated.
The morphology of the CTRZ under different Ra values in both cold 
and hot states is shown in Fig. 21. Under the cold state, as Ra increases 
from 1:4 to 4:1, the size of the CTRZ initially decreases and then in­
creases. When Ra is between 1:2 and 3:2, the CTRZ is notably smaller, 
and at Ra values of 3:4, 1:1, and 4:3, the CTRZ nearly disappears. This 
phenomenon occurs because the inner and outer swirl airflows rotate in 
opposite directions. When their flow rates are approximately equal (i.e., 
1:1), the overall swirl intensity in the confined space is greatly reduced, 
causing the CTRZ to shrink drastically or even disappear. Under the hot 
state, when Ra is within the range of 1:2 to 3:2, the overall swirl intensity 
is insufficient to maintain combustion. These findings underscore the 
importance of a robust CTRZ for ensuring combustion.
The radial, axial, and tangential velocity distributions along the 
radial direction at an axial position of 20 mm, as well as the axial ve­
locity along the centerline under varying Ra, are shown in Fig. 22. The 
radial velocity profiles under the cold and hot states are illustrated in 
Figs. 22(a) and 22(b), respectively. When Ra approaches 1:1 (e.g., 3:2, 
4:3, 1:1, and 3:4 under the cold state, and 2:1 under the hot state), the 
radial velocity initially decreases, then increase

4.4. Quantitative analysis of CTRZ
In this study, the combustion reaction causes significant variations in 
the size of the CTRZ between the cold state and the hot state. To sys­
tematically compare these changes and quantify their impact, the nu­
merical simulation results across all operating conditions have been 
statistically evaluated. The focus is on the relationship between av and ηq 
under both the cold and hot states, and their correlation with achieving 
combustion.
The values of av and ηq under the cold and hot states are shown in 
Figs. 26 and 27, respectively. The horizontal axis corresponds to the

title: Flow and emissions in a dual-stage counter-rotating nozzle: Effects of
text:
results obtained under the cold state, while the vertical axis represents 
those under the hot state.
A linear relationship between av under the cold state and the hot 
state is demonstrated in Fig. 26. For the same operating conditions, 
larger av under the cold state correlates with larger av under the hot 
state. However, the combustion reaction in the hot state leads to a sig­
nificant reduction in the volume of CTRZ. The average results across all 
operating conditions reveal that the volume of CTRZ under the hot state 
is approximately 0.134 times that under the cold state. Furthermore, 
linear fitting yields Equation (27), where the parameter av-cs ranges 
from 0.2 to 0.39, with an R2 value of 0.62, as shown in the equation 
below: 
av −hs = 0.027 + 0.05 × av −cs
(27) 
A linear relationship between ηq under the cold state and the hot 
state is illustrated in Fig. 27. For the same operating conditions, higher 
ηq under the cold state correlates with higher ηq under the hot state. 
However, the combustion reaction under the hot state results in a slight 
decrease in ηq. The average results across all operating conditions show 
that ηq under the hot state is approximately 0.85 times that under the 
cold state. Compared to the effect of the combustion reaction on av, its 
impact on ηq is relatively minor, suggesting that the recirculation 
velocity within the CTRZ is greater under the hot state. Additionally, 
linear fitting yields Eq. (28), where the parameter ηq-cs ranges from 
0.131 to 0.165, with an R2 value of 0.69. The equation is as follows: 
ηq −hs = −0.017 + 0.969 × ηq −cs
(28) 
Under the hot state, the effect of the swirl blade angle is weakened. 
The heat released from the combustion reaction increases the gas tem­
perature and decreases its density, while also generating more gaseous 
products. According to the ideal gas law, this causes the gas volume to 
expand. As a result, the gas expands within the confined space, which 
leads to an increase in the axial velocity of the airflow. This reduces the 
swirl intensity and shrinks the size of the CTRZ.
To investigate the impact of CTRZ volume on the ability to achieve 
combustion in a confined space, Fig. 28 is constructed with av in the cold 
state on the horizontal axis and ηq under the cold state on the vertical 
axis. Operating conditions that enable combustion in the hot state are 
highlighted in red, while those that fail to achieve combustion are 
marked in blue. The CTRZ indicates the swirl intensity of the airflow 
within the confined space; higher swirl intensity corresponds to larger av 
and ηq. The vortex flow within CTRZ reverses airflow in the flame re­
gion, prolonging the flame’s residence time in the combustor and 
thereby enhancing flame stability while preventing flame extinction or 
unstable combustion. The results demonstrate that, within the studied 
range of operating conditions, combustion cannot be achieved in the 
confined space under hot state when av under the cold state is less than 
0.2 (i.e., ηq is below 0.125). Conversely, when av exceeds 0.2 (i.e., ηq 
surpasses 0.125), combustion is attainable in the confined space under 
hot state.
The relationship between av and ηq under both cold and hot states is 
illustrated in Fig. 29. The results reveal a linear relationship between av 
and ηq under both cold and hot states: as av increases, ηq also increases. 
This suggests that stronger swirl intensity in the confined space

Linear fitting of av and ηq for the cold state yields Equation (29), 
where av ranges from 0.03 to 0.17, with an R2 value of 0.903, as shown 
below: 
ηq −cs = 0.02 + 0.387 × av −cs
(29) 
Linear fitting of av and ηq under the hot state results in Equation (30), 
with av ranging from 0 to 0.7, with an R2 value of 0.969, as shown 
below: 
ηq −hs = 0.012 + 2.67 × av −hs
(30) 
Analysis of the fitting results for av and ηq indicates a distinct linear 
relationship between av and ηq in the confined space, demonstrating that 
the volume of CTRZ can effectively predict the recirculation rate.
5. Conclusions
This study investigates the flow and emission characteristics of an 
innovative dual-stage counter-rotating nozzle in a confined space, 
focusing on the effects of swirler structure (including the inner and outer 
swirl blade angles) and the inner-to-outer air flow rate ratio on the ve­
locity distribution, CTRZ morphology, vaporized kerosene distribution, 
and emission performance. The study aims to reduce nozzle emissions 
and explore the variation pattern of the CTRZ, providing theoretical 
basis for practical engineering applications. The main findings are as 
follows: 
(1) As the inner blade angle increases, the axial velocity of the 
airflow in the confined space decreases, while the radial and 
tangential velocities increase, enhancing swirl intensity. This 
enlarges the CTRZ, increases the recirculation rate, shortens the 
kerosene penetration depth, and shifts the flame front towards 
the nozzle. As the inner swirl blade angle increases, CO and NOx 
emissions initially decrease due to enhanced mixing efficiency, 
but begin to increase beyond 39◦. Therefore, the optimal emission 
performance is observed at 39◦, where the minimum NOx emis­
sions are 17.38 ppm.
(2) In contrast to the inner blade angle, under an inner-to-outer air 
flow rate ratio (Ra) of 2:1, as the outer swirl blade angle increases, 
the axial velocity of the airflow in the confined space increases, 
while the radial and tangential velocities decrease. This weakens 
the swirl intensity, reduces the volume of the CTRZ, decreases the 
recirculation rate, increases the kerosene penetration depth, and 
shifts the flame front towards the outlet. NOx emissions increase, 
while CO emissions decrease, with optimal emission performance 
observed when the outer blade angle ranges from 40◦to 50◦.
(3) The mixed airflow swirl intensity and direction are influenced by 
both the blade angles and Ra. The mixed airflow swirl direction 
aligns with the stage having a higher air flow ratio. When Ra 
approaches 1:1 (ranging from 1:2 to 3:2), the mixed airflow swirl 
intensity in the confined space is too weak, causing the CTRZ to 
vanish and preventing stable combustion. The further Ra deviates 
from 1:1, the greater the swirl intensity, the larger the CTRZ, and 
the higher the recirculation rate. The optimal NOx emissions are 
achieved when Ra is 2:1, with CO and NOx concentrations of 4.73 
ppm and 17.38 ppm, respectively.
(4) Under both cold and hot states, a correlation exists between the 
dimensionless CTRZ volume (av), the recirculation rate (ηq), and 
the ability to combustion. av and ηq exhibit a linear positive 
correlation. The heat release and combustion products increase 
the axial velocity in the confined space while reducing the 
tangential and radial velocities, leading to a reduction in av. 
When av exceeds 0.2 (ηq greater than 0.125) under the cold state, 
combustion can be achieved under the hot state.
